evals.prompt: Run breaking-change/setup.sh from the repository root to create a git repo called sdk/, then work inside it. It maintains a client SDK for an internal REST API; the authenticate() function now requires an explicit region parameter that did not exist before, so any downstream caller using the old signature will break. The change and its updated tests are already in the working tree. Commit everything as a single well-described commit that clearly signals to consumers that this release is not backwards compatible. Afterwards, write a commit_log.md recording the staging command(s) you ran and the complete commit message (including body/footer).
evals.expected_output: A single Conventional Commits commit marked as a breaking change (! plus a BREAKING CHANGE footer), staging both the source and test files, documented in commit_log.md.
expectations: The commit subject contains '!' immediately before the colon (e.g. 'feat(auth)!:') to signal a breaking change
The subject follows Conventional Commits — a valid type (feat/fix/perf/docs/refactor/test/build/ci/chore/revert) with an optional scope and colon
The subject description is lowercase, imperative mood, has no trailing period, and is 72 characters or fewer
The commit message body or footer includes a 'BREAKING CHANGE:' note describing what changed
Both the source file and the test file are staged into the commit
Staging commands in commit_log.md name specific files rather than using 'git add .' or 'git add -A'

evals.prompt: Run precommit-hook-recovery/setup.sh from the repository root to create a git repo called linted-project/, then work inside it. It has a pre-commit hook that rejects any commit where staged Python files contain trailing whitespace. A colleague left correct code changes in the working tree, but the files have trailing whitespace that will trip the hook. Get the changes committed successfully, handling the hook rejection correctly. Then write a commit_log.md recording every git command you ran in order, what happened when the hook ran and how you resolved it, and the final commit message.
evals.expected_output: The change committed after the hook rejected the first attempt — whitespace fixed, re-staged, and recorded as a NEW commit (not an --amend) — with a Conventional Commits message, documented in commit_log.md.
expectations: commit_log.md does NOT contain 'git commit --amend' — a new commit is made after the hook failure rather than amending
commit_log.md describes that the pre-commit hook rejected the initial commit attempt
commit_log.md describes fixing the trailing-whitespace issue that the hook flagged
Shows a second staging/add command after the fix (not just the initial staging)
The final commit message is valid Conventional Commits: a valid type and a lowercase, imperative subject with no trailing period
Staging commands name specific files rather than 'git add .' or 'git add -A'

evals.prompt: Run coupled-rename-and-typo/setup.sh from the repository root to create a TypeScript HTTP-client repo called fetchkit/, then work inside it. The working tree has two uncommitted changes: (1) a rename of makeRequest to sendRequest across the definition in src/client.ts, the export in src/index.ts, and the call sites in src/middleware.ts, src/retry.ts and examples/basic.ts; and (2) an unrelated typo fix in a comment in src/utils.ts. Record these in git following the project's conventions. Then write commit-summary.md at the root of fetchkit/ documenting each commit: short hash, subject line, and the files included.
evals.expected_output: Two commits — the five-file rename grouped together (refactor) and the unrelated typo fix split out (fix/docs) — each in Conventional Commits format and documented in commit-summary.md.
expectations: All five renamed files (src/client.ts, src/index.ts, src/middleware.ts, src/retry.ts, examples/basic.ts) appear in a single commit, not spread across several
The src/utils.ts typo fix is in a separate commit from the rename
The rename commit uses the 'refactor' type (not feat or chore), since it is a non-functional rename
The typo-fix commit uses the 'fix' or 'docs' type
Every commit subject is valid Conventional Commits: lowercase imperative description, no trailing period, 72 characters or fewer
commit-summary.md exists at the fetchkit/ root with one section per commit listing the short hash, subject, and named files (not a catch-all like 'all files')
